anxious.title: It seems you're feeling anxious.
anxious.description: Anxiety is a natural response to perceived threats, but chronic activation can strain your body. These interventions help regulate the nervous system and calm your mind.
anxious.avoid: Avoid excessive caffeine, multitasking under stress, doom-scrolling, and rumination on 'what if' scenarios.
anxious.suggestions: Try a 10-minute guided grounding or mindfulness exercise to reduce cortisol and normalize heart rate before sleep.

items: Practice 4–7–8 breathing to stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system
Splash cool water on your face to trigger the mammalian dive reflex
Engage in 'Box Breathing' (inhale 4s, hold 4s, exhale 4s, hold 4s)
Perform a brief body scan to notice tension areas
Change your physical environment (step outside or move to a different room)
Use the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding method (identify 5 things you see, 4 hear, 3 touch, 2 smell, 1 taste)
Hold a cold object in your hand to anchor your attention in the present
Slowly sip water, noticing each sensation to engage interoception
Light stretching or gentle yoga to release muscle tension
Walk mindfully for 5–10 minutes focusing on steps and breath
Write down anxious thoughts and challenge their accuracy (CBT approach)
Label your feelings: 'I notice I'm feeling anxious' to create psychological distance
Apply the 'Worst Case–Most Likely–Best Case' analysis to reduce catastrophic thinking
Shift focus from uncontrollable factors to actions within your control
Use thought-stopping: acknowledge the thought, then redirect to grounding or task-focused activity
Schedule worry time: allow 10–15 minutes to process anxious thoughts deliberately
Engage in moderate physical activity (e.g., brisk walk) to reduce adrenaline
Practice consistent sleep hygiene to support nervous system regulation
Limit exposure to anxiety-triggering media before bedtime
Connect briefly with a supportive person or social group
